review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for reinforcement questions about why voxels were the early deep-learning entry point, why datasets mattered, and what tasks define AI plus geometry. A separate clip is unnecessary.

question_bank.question: Why were voxels the first natural representation for early 3D deep learning?
choices: They already included explicit mesh connectivity
They were easiest to adapt from 2D convolutional pipelines into 3D
They were the highest-quality representation used in graphics
question_bank.explanation: Voxels look like a 3D matrix, so researchers could extend familiar 2D convolution ideas into volumetric convolution with relatively small conceptual changes.

transcript_quotes: I just make it work on 3D matrices

question_bank.question: Why do large 3D scene datasets usually grow more slowly than object datasets?
choices: Scenes can be represented only with implicit functions
Real scenes are expensive and difficult to scan and reconstruct at scale
Scene labels are forbidden in modern benchmarks
Image supervision is unavailable for scenes
question_bank.explanation: Collecting scene-scale 3D data requires real-world capture and reconstruction, which is much costlier than aggregating large collections of synthetic object assets.

question_bank.question: Which set of tasks best matches the lecture’s broader AI-plus-geometry view of 3D learning?
choices: Only object classification and camera calibration
Generation, reconstruction, completion, recognition, and joint 2D–3D modeling
Only image captioning and language modeling
question_bank.explanation: The task space is broader than recognition alone and includes making shapes, recovering them from observations, filling in missing geometry, and combining 2D and 3D signals.
